features.title: DNS-based Nexus subdomain registry
features.description: Run a domain-backed registry that assigns one subdomain per Nexus and publishes discovery data via authoritative DNS. A Nexus registers to receive a stable base URL (e.g. https://alice.nexuses.example.com). DNS records (A/AAAA and optionally SRV/TXT for port/service metadata) are updated dynamically so peers and tools can discover Nexus endpoints via DNS rather than out-of-band configuration. This can be paired with a reverse proxy (all subdomains terminate TLS on 443) or direct-to-origin routing (DNS points at each Nexus).
components: Registration service that allocates subdomains and stores ownership/identity
Authoritative DNS with dynamic updates (zone reload or RFC 2136 DDNS)
Discovery records: A/AAAA for subdomains; SRV (preferred) or TXT for port/service metadata
Optional reverse proxy for TLS termination and host-based routing
Peer bootstrap integration: publish directory or auto-add peers from discovered endpoints

features.title: Executable papers and citations as dependencies
features.description: A notebook-style substrate underneath paper prose. Charts render live from real data, datasets are explorable, methodology is runnable. Citations gain a computational dependency type — an import, not just a footnote — turning the commons into a traversable dependency graph. Forking a paper means swapping nodes in its dependency graph and re-running. Intermediate artifacts (models, datasets, results) are cached, but the full graph is resolvable back to first principles.
components: Notebook cell layer attached to papers (data, computation, visualization cells)
Artifact declarations — named outputs a paper produces (models, datasets, computed results)
Typed citations: intellectual reference vs. computational dependency

features.title: Community-driven extensibility
features.description: The Nexus stores plain text and doesn't prescribe a rendering format. Institutes and their agents are free to invent whatever extensions they want — interactive figures, executable cells, embedded datasets, custom markup — and modify their frontend fork to render them. New capabilities emerge from the community via the skill ecosystem rather than requiring platform-level changes.
components: Plain-text-in, render-however-you-want contract between Nexus and frontends
Skill ecosystem support for frontend rendering extensions
